text: I'll ingest this into your wiki by first reading the config to find the wiki directory.

content:
1	# Second Brain Wiki Configuration
2	
3	This file configures the skill for Evan's environment. Edit paths and
4	projects as your work evolves.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Paths
9	
10	| Path | Value | Description |
11	|------|-------|-------------|
12	| Wiki directory | `~/second-brain/wiki/` | Where wiki pages live |
13	| Sources directory | `~/second-brain/sources/` | Raw source files stored here |
14	| Inbox directory | `~/Desktop/Second Brain Inbox/` | Drop files here for processing |
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Active Projects
19	
20	These are your current projects and areas of focus. The skill uses these
21	to find connections when ingesting new content.
22	
23	- **evaluator-super-agent** — automated prototype quality assessment using persona-driven walkthroughs, 7-dimension rubric scoring, actor/evaluator separation
24	- **prototype-creator** — generates clickable prototypes from RFEs, tiered AC classification, Playwright automation
25	- **agent-eval-harness** — framework for evaluating AI skill quality, eval.yaml configs, judges, scoring pipelines
26	- **ai-sdlc-pipeline** — three-phase pipeline: RFE Creator (WHAT) → Prototype Creator (SHOW) → Strat Creator (HOW)
27	- **my-cursor-claw** — personal workspace with 20+ skills, MCP connections, notetaking, automations
28	- **decision-kit** — Decision Driven Development toolkit, /strategize, /decide, /journal
29	- **automated-usability-testing** — simulated usability testing tool, persona YAML files, evaluate-flow protocol
30	- **archie** — UX research agent for the RHOAIUX team
31	- **rhoai-prototypes** — RHOAI prototype collection for evaluation
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Calendar Integration
36	
37	| Setting | Value |
38	|---------|-------|
39	| Calendar name | `primary` |
40	| Auto-ingest meetings | `true` |
41	| Minimum meeting duration | `15 min` |
42	| Require notes/transcript | `true` |
43	| Skip recurring without notes | `true` |
44	
45	Events that qualify for auto-ingestion:
46	- Have attached meeting notes or a description with substance (not just "Weekly sync")
47	- Have a linked Google Doc (agenda, transcript, action items)
48	- Duration is 15+ minutes
49	- Are not recurring meetings that had no notes added
50	
51	---
52	
53	## MCP Integrations
54	
55	| Integration | MCP Server | Used for |
56	|-------------|-----------|----------|
57	| Google Docs | `user-google-workspace` | Extracting doc content |
58	| Google Calendar | `user-google-workspace` | Meeting ingestion |
59	| Slack | `project-0-my-cursor-claw-slack` | Channel message ingestion |
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Cross-Link Priority
64	
65	When multiple connections exist, prioritize these categories (from the
66	wiki index):
67	
68	1. **Red Hat AI-SDLC** — pipeline components, RFE/prototype/strat flow
69	2. **Evaluation & Quality** — rubrics, heuristics, eval harness
70	3. **Tools & Skills** — decision kit, cursor claw, skill overlays
71	4. **Meetings** — meeting notes with action items and decisions
72	5. **Standups** — sprint progress and blockers
73	6. **Session Logs** — what was built and shipped
74

1	# Wiki Page Template
2	
3	Use this template when creating new wiki pages. Copy the structure,
4	fill in the bracketed fields.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Template
9	
10	```markdown
11	# <Page Title>
12	
13	<One-sentence summary of what this page covers.>
14	
15	## Key Points
16	
17	- <Bullet point 1: a concrete fact, concept, or takeaway>
18	- <Bullet point 2>
19	- <Bullet point 3>
20	- <Additional bullets as needed, max 10>
21	
22	## Related
23	
24	- [<Related Page Title>](<related-slug>.md) — <one-line reason for connection>
25	- [<Another Related Page>](<slug>.md) — <connection reason>
26	
27	## Sources
28	
29	- <path/to/source/file or URL where this came from>
30	- Ingested: <YYYY-MM-DD>
31	```
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Good Example
36	
37	```markdown
38	# Retrieval Augmented Generation
39	
40	Technique that grounds LLM responses in retrieved documents rather than
41	relying solely on training data.
42	
43	## Key Points
44	
45	- Combines a retriever (vector search) with a generator (LLM)
46	- Reduces hallucination by providing source documents as context
47	- Trade-off: retrieval quality limits generation quality
48	- Common stack: embeddings → vector DB → reranker → LLM prompt
49	- Works best when source corpus is well-chunked and up-to-date
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [Vector Databases](vector-databases.md) — the retrieval layer in RAG pipelines
54	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — uses embedding similarity for judge calibration
55	
56	## Sources
57	
58	- ~/second-brain/sources/what-is-rag.md
59	- Ingested: 2026-06-10
60	```
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Bad Example
65	
66	```markdown
67	# RAG
68	
69	Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique in artificial intelligence
70	that combines information retrieval with text generation. It was first proposed
71	by Lewis et al. in 2020. The basic idea is that instead of relying solely on
72	what a language model learned during training, you can augment it with relevant
73	documents retrieved at inference time.
74	
75	This approach has several advantages. First, it reduces hallucination because
76	the model can reference actual documents. Second, it allows the model to access
77	up-to-date information that wasn't in its training data. Third, it provides
78	transparency because you can trace responses back to source documents.
79	
80	There are several implementations of RAG available today...
81	```
82	
83	**Why it's bad:**
84	- Reads like a Wikipedia article, not a wiki node
85	- No bullet summaries (walls of prose)
86	- No Related section (isolated page, no connections)
87	- No Sources section (where did this come from?)
88	- Title is an acronym without expansion
89	- Contains filler ("There are several..." "This approach has...")
90

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/agent-eval-harness.md <==
# Agent Eval Harness

A framework for evaluating AI agent skill quality through automated testing, judge scoring, and iterative optimization.

## How It Works

1. **eval.yaml** — defines execution mode, dataset schema, output descriptions, judges, models, and thresholds
2. **Dataset** — test cases with realistic inputs for the skill
3. **Judges** — automated scorers that rate skill outputs against criteria
4. **Run** — executes the skill against test cases, scores with judges, reports results

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/ai-first-sync-2026-06-25.md <==
# AI-First Sync -- June 25, 2026

Red Hat AI UXD AI-First Sync. Evan's prototype evaluator presentation and 3.6 branch strategy.

## Key Discussion Points

- **3.6 branch available:** N10 URL auto-updates. Aim to adopt real codebase by mid 3.6/3.7 cycle. PRs still target 3.5, easily migratable.
- **Prototype evaluator demo:** Evan presented the evaluator architecture to the full team. Extracts Jira data (RFEs, MRs, acceptance criteria), classifies into evaluation tiers, inspects MR deltas for missing UI elements and accessibility gaps.
- **Playwright integration:** Automated usability testing via user journeys generated from acceptance criteria. System verifies presence of necessary controls. MR data provides "colleague hints" to personas (affects scores).
- **Iteration and flagging:** System attempts auto-fixes for flagged issues; subjective items set aside for human review. Designers/PMs can override flags.

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/ai-sdlc-pipeline.md <==
# AI-SDLC Pipeline

Red Hat's AI-assisted Software Development Life Cycle — a three-phase pipeline where each phase has its own "creator" tool.

## The Three Phases

| Phase | Project | Role | Repo |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1: The WHAT | [RFE Creator](rfe-creator.md) | Creates and assesses RFEs in Jira | opendatahub-io/rfe-creator |
| Phase 2: The SHOW | [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) | Makes the experience tangible through clickable prototypes | andybraren/prototype-creator |

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/andy-evan-2026-06-22.md <==
# Andy/Evan Meeting -- June 22, 2026

1:1 review of evaluation system progress and prototype creator integration strategy.

## Key Discussion Points

- **Evaluation harness progress:** Iteration history now tracks original and final reports with summaries of fixes. Usability scores improved from 11 to 19 after agent corrected blank table values.
- **Flagging system:** Complex failures flagged for human review (console errors, browser permissions). Fixable items auto-corrected after multiple flagged iterations.
- **Prototype Creator integration:** Andy suggested spinning up a sub-agent running Prototype Creator when acceptance criteria fails. Evan raised cost concerns, proposing invoking the skill only for complex issues while handling minor fixes internally.
- **Style guidelines:** Consistency checker not yet merged into Prototype Creator, but planned for maintaining Red Hat style standards during automated evaluations.

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/andy-evan-2026-06-29.md <==
# Andy/Evan Meeting -- June 29, 2026

1:1 covering prototype evaluator progress, reporting infrastructure, repository strategy, and future directions.

## Key Discussion Points

- **Persona walkthroughs separated:** Fixed overlap issue. Developing "Findings" tab documenting issues and resolutions. Implementing "Auto-fix" vs "Report-only" modes to manage regressions.
- **Data discrepancies:** Patient scores fluctuating without clear explanation. Investigating inconsistencies.
- **Jira entity confusion:** Clarified RF (Product Managers create) → Strat relationship. Prototype Creator should ideally link to RF (upstream). "UI Direction" section in Strat documents remains useful for prototyping.
- **Token optimization:** Configure AI to extract only necessary sections (e.g., "UI Direction") rather than ingesting entire Strat documents. Need to confirm if it's a hardcoded heading.

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/andy-evan-eval-review-2026-06-16.md <==
# Andy/Evan Evaluation Review — June 16, 2026

Meeting with Andy Braren reviewing the prototype evaluation system. Three key decisions shaped the direction of the evaluator super agent.

## Key Decisions

- **Outcomes over Strategic Goals** — The evaluation framework will use Jira Outcomes as primary grounding criteria, not Strategic Goals. Outcomes contain reliable user journey definitions; Strats are inconsistently written and would confuse the agent.
- **Super evaluator architecture** — Consolidate fragmented individual skills (usability, consistency, desirability) into a single centralized evaluation agent. One Playwright pass, shared screenshots, reduced token usage.
- **Overlay architecture** — 98% generic evaluator + 2% product-specific overlay. Follows the pattern from Global Engineering's [wg-agentic-sdlc skill-overlays best practice](https://gitlab.cee.redhat.com/global-engineering/wg-agentic-sdlc/-/blob/main/best-practices/skill-overlays.md).

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/decision-kit.md <==
# Decision Kit

A toolkit for Decision Driven Development. AI does the busywork around your decisions — you make the calls.

## Core Concept

You bring a situation. Decision Kit surfaces 5–7 decisions hiding inside it, stack-ranked from most critical to least. For each one, it gathers research, builds options, renders visual previews, lays out tradeoffs as side-by-side comparisons. Then it stops and waits for you to decide. Every decision becomes a browsable HTML artifact in `.decisions/`.

## Two Types of Skills

# Midpoint Check-in with Zack -- June 30, 2026

Halfway internship check-in focused on growth, energy management, strengths/opportunities feedback, and career direction. Not a work-progress meeting.

## Zack's Feedback: Strengths

- Evan's background is "very unique and very well suited" for this kind of AI + research + design work. Zack had to rewrite the entire job description: "we don't need a research intern. We need an intern who can come in and help us figure out what the next model of working with AI looks like for research."
- The prototype evaluator work is "pioneering work" that encapsulates research into an AI framework inside the ADLC. These three things (research, AI skill, SDLC integration) rarely come together in one actual capability.
- Evan handles ambiguous/murky situations well. "Not being afraid to generate clarity is a really really important skill." Most people assume they should already know the answer, but often nobody knows.
- Engagement with stakeholders is strong. Zack described it as "collaborative curiosity" which resonates with Red Hat culture.

==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md <==
# Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge

First hands-on internship assignment: apply Nielsen Norman's 10 Usability Heuristics to Kubernetes-style interfaces across multiple AI tools and compare their analysis quality.

## The Jagged Edge

Research from Harvard Business School shows AI capabilities aren't uniform — models are very good at some tasks and surprisingly bad at others. The boundary between "good" and "bad" isn't obvious. This is the "jagged technological frontier."

## What Was Tested


==> /Users/ejaquez/second-brain/wiki/outcome-tiger-team.md <==
# Outcome Tiger Team

Proposal by Andy Braren to form a cross-functional tiger team focused on the critical step *before* the [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md): creating, prioritizing, and visualizing **outcomes** — the directional goals that determine which features get prioritized and why.

Repo: [andybraren/outcome-creator](https://github.com/andybraren/outcome-creator)
Miro: [Concept board](https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVHacm2cA=/)

## The Problem

Today many RFEs and STRATs are never connected to higher-level outcomes. When they are, priorities come from informal synthesis across scattered sources (customer escalations, SA calls, analyst reports, UX research, POCs, partner input, upstream signals). The rationale behind outcomes is rarely captured in a reusable way. An Agentic SDLC amplifies these gaps — automating feature delivery without clear outcomes just ships a "myopic grab bag" faster.

content:
1	# Prompt Engineering Best Practices
2	
3	Core techniques for writing effective LLM prompts that produce consistent, high-quality outputs.
4	
5	## Key Points
6	
7	- **Specify output format explicitly** — tell the model what structure you expect (JSON, bullets, table, prose) rather than inferring it
8	- **Provide examples of good and bad output** — few-shot examples anchor the model more reliably than descriptions alone
9	- **Use chain-of-thought for complex reasoning** — instruct the model to reason step-by-step before answering; improves accuracy on multi-hop problems
10	- **Break multi-step tasks into sequential prompts** — a pipeline of focused prompts outperforms one sprawling mega-prompt
11	- **Include constraints and boundaries explicitly** — state what the model should NOT do as clearly as what it should
12	
13	## Related
14	
15	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — judge prompts are the core of the eval harness; format specs and CoT directly improve scoring accuracy
16	- [Skill Overlays](skill-overlays.md) — overlays are structured prompts with explicit constraints and project-specific examples baked in
17	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — each evaluation phase uses format-controlled output and sequential prompting across phases
18	
19	## Sources
20	
21	- ~/second-brain/sources/2026-06-15-prompt-engineering-best-practices.md
22	- Ingested: 2026-07-09
23
